candidate_problems.text: What precise structural conditions are necessary and sufficient for a hierarchy-preserving equilibrium to emerge without explicit collusion? The conjecture names repeated interaction, visibility of defection, retaliation capacity, and long horizons, but it remains open how these variables trade off, whether there are threshold effects, and which combinations actually generate stable self-perpetuation versus merely temporary conformity.

How can one distinguish decentralized incentive-driven reproduction of power from explicit collusion, shared ideology, or simple selection effects in real cases? The conjecture is explanatory, but its strongest unresolved tension is empirical identifiability: what observable signatures would uniquely indicate that structure-induced repeated-game dynamics, rather than conspiracy or preference alignment, are doing the causal work?

What breaks the equilibrium, and under what extreme conditions does individually rational loyalty flip into individually rational defection? The conjecture implies that anonymity, one-shot interaction, shorter horizons, or weaker gatekeeping should destabilize domination, but it is unresolved which interventions are actually high-leverage, whether disruption requires coordinated shocks, and how alternative equilibria form instead of the old structure simply reconstituting itself.
